question: What is the docs' general rule of thumb when choosing between custom glue code and an existing connector?

options.text: Prefer custom glue code unless you have a good reason — such as a complex integration with little customization needed — to use a connector.
Always prefer connectors, because they are easier to test than custom glue code.
Always extract a reusable connector before writing any glue code, so integration logic is never duplicated.
Use a connector for every external system to avoid running any additional services next to the application.

a.text: The docs say: as a general rule of thumb, prefer custom glue code whenever you don't have a good reason to go with an existing connector — and they cite complex integrations with little customization (e.g., the RPA bridge) as a good reason for a connector.

b.text: The docs say the opposite about testing: a downside of connectors is that testing gets harder because you cannot easily hook in mocks.

c.text: The docs explicitly warn against extracting connectors too early, since they become hard to adjust once in production and reused across applications.

d.text: A documented downside of connectors is that the connector itself must be operated alongside your application, so reaching for one for every system is not the recommendation.

explanation: The best-practices recommendation defaults to custom glue code. Connectors earn their keep for complex but low-customization integrations (the RPA bridge, AWS Lambda) or reusable generic adapters; otherwise the operational overhead, limited extensibility, and harder testability tip the balance back toward writing code in your language of choice.

docs.title: Connecting the workflow engine with your world
